sanskrit_devanagari:
सुखदुःखे समे कृत्वा लाभालाभौ जयाजयौ ।
ततो युद्धाय युज्यस्व नैवं पापमवाप्स्यसि ॥ ३८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
sukha-duḥkhe same kṛtvā lābhālābhau jayājayau |
tato yuddhāya yujyasva naivaṁ pāpam avāpsyasi || 38 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; line-break inserted between hemistichs)
exact (text body identical; uses ': ' for visarga in display layer; underlying akṣara identical)
exact (IAST text body identical; plain transliteration without diacritics)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.038 = MBh_06,024.038). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: Do thou fight for the sake of fighting, without considering happiness or distress, loss or gain, victory or defeat – and by so doing you shall never incur sin.
Fight for the sake of duty, treating alike happiness and distress, loss and gain, victory and defeat. Fulfilling your responsibility in this way, you will never incur sin.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Lord Kṛṣṇa now directly says that Arjuna should fight for the sake of fighting because He desires the battle. There is no consideration of happiness or distress, profit or loss, victory or defeat in the activities of Kṛṣṇa consciousness. That everything should be performed for the sake of Kṛṣṇa is transcendental consciousness; so there is no reaction to material activities.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.38 As regards that, listen to this advice for you then you are engaged in battle considering it to be your duty: Krtva, treating; sukha-duhkhe, happiness and sorrow; same, with equanimity, i.e. without having likes and dislikes; so also treating labha-alabhau, gain and loss; jaya-ajayau, conquest and defeat, as the same; tatah, then; yuddhaya yujyasva, engage in battle.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 2.38 as the FIRST explicit statement of equanimity (samatva) as the disposition required for action that does not bind. The three pairs (sukha/duḥkha, lābha/alābha, jaya/ajaya) are not collapsed — they are treated as the same (same kṛtvā), which is different from being made the same. The actor remains aware that pleasure differs from pain, gain from loss, victory from defeat; the equanimity is the actor's stance toward the pairs, not the erasure of the pairs. Shankara also notes the verse's bracketed status: it is incidental to the main metaphysical argument (which is about realisation, not action), but sets up the explicit yoga-of-action doctrine that 2.39 will name.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.38 Thus, knowing the self to be eternal, different from the body and untouched by all corporeal qualities, remaining unaffected by pleasure and pain resulting from the weapon-strokes etc., inevitable in a war, as also by gain and loss of wealth, victory and defeat, and keeping yourself free from attachment to heaven and such other frutis, begin the battle considering it merely as your own duty.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads 2.38 as the synthesis: knowledge of the self (sāṅkhya, 2.11-2.30) becomes the basis on which one can act 'unaffected' by the three pairs. The pleasure/pain pair is corporeal (body-level), gain/loss is economic, victory/defeat is socio-political — three different registers, all of which the actor passes through but is not changed by. Ramanuja explicitly calls 'pāpam' here 'transmigratory existence which is misery' — the verse is not about avoiding moral fault, it is about not creating bondage. After 2.38, Krishna 'begins to expound the Yoga of work' (i.e., 2.39 onwards). Ramanuja's reading positions 2.38 as the seed-statement of the doctrine that 2.47 will state in its mature form.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Regarding as equal pleasure and pain, loss and gain and also the cause of both these dualities which is victory or defeat. The attribute of equanimity is absolute freedom from elation and despondency. Giving up all notions of what is pleasurable, being equipoised by whatever comes of its own accord and by fighting the battle as a matter of ksatriya duty Arjuna will not incur any sin.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Sridhara names samatva as 'absolute freedom from elation and despondency' — the dispositional state, not a doctrine. He notes the structure: pleasure/pain and gain/loss are the surface dualities; victory/defeat is named as the cause of those dualities (i.e., the deeper layer that produces the felt experience). The actor takes the same disposition toward all three. Sridhara also makes the negation explicit: 'pāpam' here is the sin of NOT acting — refraining from the battle. The equanimity does not produce sin; the failure to engage from equanimity does.

positions.rendering: without considering happiness or distress, loss or gain, victory or defeat
treating alike happiness and distress, loss and gain, victory and defeat
treating with equanimity (samatva), without having likes and dislikes; the pairs remain pairs, the actor's stance toward them is the same
remaining unaffected by three different registers (corporeal, economic, socio-political); knowledge of the self is the basis of the unaffectedness
absolute freedom from elation and despondency; victory/defeat is the cause of pleasure/pain and gain/loss, so all three pairs collapse into a single dispositional move

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The doctrinal stake: is the actor erasing the pairs (treating them as not-different) or holding the pairs while taking the same stance toward each? Prabhupada's 'without considering' suggests near-erasure. Mukundananda's 'treating alike' is structurally truer: the pairs are kept, the actor's response is the same. Shankara explicitly says the disposition is 'without likes and dislikes' — i.e., without rāga and dveṣa toward the pairs, not without recognition of the pairs. The distinction matters because the verse is the seed of equanimity-doctrine: if the pairs are erased, the actor cannot read feedback (pleasure/pain) or measure outcomes (gain/loss) or update on results (victory/defeat). The verse instead requires the actor to read all three honestly while not being moved-into-bondage by them. This is the doctrinal seed of 2.47 (do the action, do not grasp the fruit) and 2.48 (yoga-stha kuru karmāṇi, established in yoga, do actions).

literal_meaning: Treating happiness and unhappiness as the same, gain and loss, victory and defeat — then engage in battle. Thus you will not incur sin.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The first explicit appearance of the equanimity doctrine (samatva) in the Gita and the doctrinal seed of the karma-yoga teaching that 2.39-2.51 elaborates. Three pairs are named: sukha/duḥkha (felt-experience), lābha/alābha (gain/loss), jaya/ajaya (victory/defeat). The actor treats all three pairs as the same — 'same kṛtvā' — which the commentators read as 'with the same dispositional stance,' not as 'as if they were not different.' The pairs remain pairs; the actor's stance toward them is the same. Once that disposition is achieved, engagement in action (yuddhāya yujyasva) does not produce bondage (pāpam, read by Ramanuja as 'transmigratory existence,' i.e., bondage rather than moral fault). The verse is structurally critical: 2.39 names the two methods (sāṅkhya and yoga); 2.47 gives the mature statement; 2.48 gives the imperative form ('yoga-stha kuru karmāṇi'). 2.38 contains all of these in seed. Shankara reads it as 'incidental' to the main metaphysical argument but as the explicit setup for the yoga-of-action exposition. Ramanuja reads it as the synthesis of self-knowledge with right action.
